column2: Tide Goes In, Tide Goes Out, You Can't Explain That!
column36:
Managing Thanos was easy in the first game as only one master strike really came free to harm the field. It didn't take anything too threatening and the Ultimate spiderman/Elektra side got the benefit of the doubt of the first scheme twist and got to pick up 4-5 heroes in a row. 

Forge And Domino had struggling issues getting themselves together but the Spider-Squad was able to effectively draw their whole deck with Phasing Lockjaw and use Elektra's 6 drop combined with Spidey's rare to get 30+ attack on the final turn. 

Game 2 went smoother for Forge and Domino to really combine with Fantastic's rare to deliver strikes to the Mastermind effectively almost each turn. Unfortunately back to back Master Strikes took away the Spider rare and stopped that deck from gaining any ground. 

This meant we had a bit of a slower round overall with 22 on game 1 versus 25 on game 2. Opting for the shorter turn but wanted to showcase the power of both decks and how versatile they can be no matter the circumstance.

First game went very well, and played the second game to see if there was any room to improve.  I knew by turn 10 of that second game that it wouldn't be better, thought it wasn't as worse as I thought it might have been at the time.

With two free heroes to select from Dark City, even Poison Thanos became rather easy! For my first attempt I selected Iceman and Bishop. My plan: One 2-Cost-Deck and hoping for the rare 2-cost card and one "blue" Iceman-wall! But only Iceman and Bishop performed as they often perform! Was unlucky that 2 consecutive Strikes stole two 7-cost-cards from my hand! But I had a good and clean win in 31 turns. 
But then I realized, that the Poison-Villains didn't gave me much VP. So I swapped Bishop with Jean Grey for my second attempt. And altough I needed 31 turns again I had a much better result thanks to almost 20 rescued Bystanders! X-Men rule! ;-)
Now hoping to climb up the ladder again for the big showdown!

Both games went well, but second was a little faster and more PPT. P1 built mainly Iceman and FFU and got Mr Fantastic. P2 built mainly Ultimate Spidey, Wolverine and Domino, picking up Against All Odds. Twists helping get a quick build from all the cheap cards for P2. Got lucky with Strike timing so that Poison Thanos only had +2 most of the game, and never above +3. I think I had nearly all copies of Marvel Team-Up in deck 2, so was speeding through the Sidekick stack.

column36: One Side went full spidey while the other abused versatile and double-symbol cards. Spidey went hard and did a great job keeping the city clear and then one MASSIVE turn that resulted in an initial count of 29-28 Recruit-Fight from Proxima Midnight and Mr. Fantastic's rare cards (plus a bunch of other stuff) managed to hit Poison Thanos 3 times AND gave me an extra villain in the Pile. Great Stuff!
Great challenge.  Keeping Thanos from powering it up was tough.

Was not excited to see Poison Thanos as I can recall my frustration with him last tournament. He can be so annoyingly hard to play against, and the difficulty is very dependent on the luck of when Master Strikes occur. Graciously, this time around was much more tolerable given tools available in the setup. The Negative Pulses helped manage the city, and the heroes provided led to some strong builds. With as hard as Poison Thanos can be to find, I went for maximum cheese with Forge and Namor, hoping to at least make progress on Thanos with their rare cards. One deck was black and versatile (the much stronger deck in both games), and the other deck was green and yellow. Rare Mr. Fantastic was the all-star in the tech deck which was backed up with Domino's rare and Forge. I had a turn where I hit a 14-strength Thanos three times because I chained together 14+ attack and 20+ recruit which I Focused into MM attack with Mr. F. I ended up not cheesing much (if at all maybe) in game one, but I managed to do so with Forge and Namor in the second game (which was a win but worse result). Kudos to the setup this time around for making Poison Thanos much more tolerable. The difficulty is still very dependent on when the Master Strikes occur and if the last Scheme Twist is high in the deck, but the Scheme helping on half the twists and only having 9 twists (rather than 10+) when Evil Wins on ST 9 helped a ton. I'm still going to groan when I see Poison Thanos in the future, but I'll just be sure to bring out my big freaking guns again, literally.

This was a fun one! One of us focused on <2 Spider build, other with tons of 4 cards and Domino's rare in first game.  The spider build never really got going, though, so it took a while to win.  Second game the spider build went way better, but we had a pretty unlucky villian deck and lost that game with one MM hit to go.

Amadeus Cho and the Cuckoos worked very well together, both sides built Outwit/Tactical formation draw engines.  Only complaint (and this is very strange coming from me) was the VD moved a little slowly so would've liked a bit more VP but beggars can't be choosers I suppose.

DFTG subbing for ESC & Son: Managed to add 0.07 PPT with my 2nd attempt. Same turn count, escapes etc.

Deceptively tough setup. The Scheme always seemed to take the cards we needed at the wrong time. We waffled between flooding the HQ with Tech or running Bystanders with Hiroim and Jean Grey, but decided for Tech to play more new stuff my partner hadn't played. Pretty fun, though.

This was very swingy in terms of fun level really depends how well our heroes came out. 

Barely won with a double mastermind kill right before the scheme twist flipped on it second round, opted to use that because better turns and less escapes

We lost the first game due to Scheme Twists coming out faster than normal. We decided to run it back cause we thought our heroes were good just the time ticked too fast on us. In the 2nd game the Twists were also coming out fast, so it wasn't looking good for us. But we stuck with it, trying to focus on hitting General Ross whenever we could. Eventually we were able to get him before we ran out of time. Losing cards with each Twist was always a bummer on this one. Luckily game 2 gave us enough time to win.

Both attempts were a win, but both in dramatically different ways. In attempt 1 I managed to have a recruit strong deck 1, with Polaris' rare as first rare early on. Which helped a lot to recruit the pawns to weaken Professor X. Eventually that helped to make Professor X hittable by both decks. Piercing energy helped clear some Shi'ar Death Commandos, but I never got enough to hit anything serious with it. Attempt 1 took 31 turns, so was quite slow as well. Due to getting Thano's' rare and Longshot's rare from the Pawns, deck 1 could beat Professor X easily near the end, although always just short of hitting him 2 times in 1 turn, short some small amount of attack. Thanos' recruit cards were pretty strong as well, providing the coveted recruit to be able to get the pawns away from Professor X.

In Attempt 1 my plan was to shuffle the Twists back in from twists 3 and on. This worked, but was sometimes crippling for the turns that the plots showed up. In attempt 2 my plan was to do the same, but I got lucky with a few early Enchantress' 4-costs. This together with KO's from villains in the CIty helped me get my deck 2 to an insane end product of 7-8 cards with piercing energy, the final turn I got more piercing energy than I could spend (15!). Banshee's Uncommon was key to getting his rare, but I also needed Enchantress' 4-cost to KO her other 4 cost for it. It took a couple turns of planning to get there, but from turn 14 on my deck was so thinned out that I was guaranteed at least 6 piercing energy, even with the worst draw. While improving my deck into insane PE levels, Professor X could be hit as well. Clearing the City was necessary then still as well, so I hit Professor x on turn 18, 20, twice in 22 and Final Blow in 24. Deck 1 was a joke in that game, attempting to go for Longshot's cards, but it didn't end up working well.

It was well that Piercing Energy worked so well in attempt 2 as well, because the pawns only kept growing in numbers and beating Professor X with normal attack would have proven too hard. The Twists weren't as crippling in attempt 2, as it was a much faster game, but the feeling of preventing the 5th toxin gave the last couple turns a strong tension. Winning depended on the shuffling back of the twist on turn 23 more than a good enough hand for turn 24, as that deck was near perfect by then.

X-Men Danger Room Goes Berserk is a tough scheme, depending on your luck, but thematically it suits Professor X fine. This being a Villains game gave us Madame Hydras instead of S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers and New recruits instead of sidekicks. New recruits were underwhelming btu Madame Hydra is dodgeable, so not clogging up your deck/hand in the endgame. I found this setup not as hard as last week's setup, but I sort of 'cheated' with piercing energy. I can imagine that hitting Professor X without strong and recruit granting heroes can be very hard, depending on the shuffle luck of both the Hero Deck and the Villain Deck. Longshot, while being useful for Feasting safely, did not grant recruit.

Also, Enchantress is my favourite Hero, and while she did not shine in attempt 1, I strongly love her 4 and 6-cost cards. Her 4-cost made the thinning to 0 starters possible quite fast in attempt 2. I simply love the achievement feeling of thinning one, or sometimes even both, deck(s) from all the starter troopers and agents. The strength of the non-grey cards then shines through more as well, of course. Thanks to Kylah for a merciful steup this week, I'm looking forward to next week's final challenge.

Fun Setup.  First game, I got my right hand deck down to just 7 cards and one was the 8-Cost Thanos who ko's bystanders and kills the mastermind or a villain with less fight than the number of KO'd bystanders.  With just 7 cards, the bodies stacked up quick.  But, I'm a bonehead and realized I was playing Human Shields as Hidden Witnesses, paying 2 recruit instead of 2 fight to kill them.  So I am submitting my second game, which still came out better with my right hand being all red, and my left hand being all blue, with lots of draw and piercing energy.  Also, that enchantress card that lets you KO cards for their cost helped deny Professor X his buffing cards.  Nice setup.

(Even after Xavier snatched the 8-cost Thanos card, which I had no chance of getting back from the pawn deck, so Xavier got stuck at 11+ power. (In the end that hand got boosted by the berserk cards of Longshot.) The second hand could not really get going during the game, but Thanos did sacrificie 2 or 3 bystanders to help keeping the city safe. (I got many villains, so almost got to escapes as well.) The scheme itself did not really matter this play session. I paid for one twist with hopes to get a villain I could hit, and let the other twist traps to go. In the end I finished with 4 toxins, but did not really matter.

This was a fun setup and worked well with most of the heroes.  Longshot was a bit of a dud.  Enchantress worked well with Namor and other Ranged cards to make deck one effective, while Lady Thor teamed up with Thanos to dominate Professor X on the second deck.  Ended up using Thanos' rare only once.

Fun setup. I don't think I chose appropriate heroes though because it was difficult to get any decent attack or momentum going.
had recruit issues noth games, and just went to time, had her on the final blow game two

I like the mechanic of the setup. It was definitely challenging. With the Master Strikes shrinking the city and the Henchmen moving pushing everything forward (then going back into the deck), it just exponentially makes every turn harder. My discard deck came together beautifully. My bystander deck dragged behind because the payoff cards were there, but the engine cards were not coming out.
I really like games that are a true race against time

column36: Wow, diabolical scheme for Galactus (not to mention the Endless Armies of HYDRA!)  HQ did not cooperate, as I saw no Angel/Cyc synergy that I was hoping for. None

I should have expected this after complaining two weeks ago about a setup being too easy and unfun. I challenged Kylah, and the bill came due. This is an evil setup and I got rolled both times. My first game, pretty early there were 3 goons followed by a scheme twist. Nothing I can do about that. And that put me behind. Plus 3 pretty early strikes shortening the city. And I played poorly, was just feeling it out. Second game, in the first 6 turns, there were 3 master strikes. I totally don't need a city for this type of scheme lmao. Went worse. Just doesn't feel fair at all. You may ask, why did I rank this as a 3 and not lower? While I think it's unfair and mean, I'm totally gonna play it a third time for my curiosity lol. So I guess I don't hate it.

column36: Pretty Basic Game

I had to. I simply had to go all in on the multiverse of Stranges on one hand, with the other hand grabbing what it could. I wound up with a couple Carnage cards in the Strange deck and some Strange cards in the other deck. But all five Heroes in the game synergized surprisingly well. Also, Wand of Watoomb is GOATed.

One scheme twist took out 3 villains (2 Kree, 1 Skrull) before I had enough attack and I had to let two more Skrull escape after just to manage the Scheme.  But it was manageable, even though I wound up with 3 Kree Twists.  Once the escapees evened out, I was in good shape to fight the Mastermind both in the Astral Plain and his home.  All the heroes played a part.  Amazed how few I needed to do the job.  The artifacts really made the difference.
Not a bad set up.  Without the Fear Lords popping into the Astral Plane and causing problems, Nightmare didn't live up to his name (and I'm OK with that).
First game was a quick bust due to too many Skrulls in the city and too many scheme twists at the start of the game. Second game was the flip so it was fun with the two extremes!
The mastermind was fun!. Didnt care for Vishanti.

As the Kree-Skrull War terrorized the city, the heroes had to split focus as they fought Nightmare in the Astral Plane. Surprisingly, it was not Doctor Strange who fought off Nightmare in the Astral Plane, but rather the symbiotes, Carnage and Venom. Instead, Strange aligned with Star-Lord to collect artifacts so that they could protect the Material Plane and fend off against Nightmare when he finally came.
